result:
The back of my throat tastes like metal this morning—copper and ozone—as though I licked a battery in a dream. It always happens when I travel too far inside my own head overnight: whole civilizations spring up among the folds of sulci, independent of me, arguing, trading, falling in love with holograms of tomorrow. I wake and they’re gone, except for the faint electric flavor lingering like the ghost of too-bright neon.

I used to keep a notebook by the bed to trap some of that traffic: rubber-banded bazaars and moons that grow milk-blue horns. But after a while I realized the notes only ever captured the census page, never the smell of alien cornbread or the sound of boots walking on volcanic glass. Memory is a sketch artist who refuses to leave the police station; imagination boards night trains and never looks back.

Today the light outside is the color of slightly over-steeped chamomile, the kind of diffuse gold that makes you think the sun is contemplating a sabbatical. Sparrows chatter like teenagers at a bus stop, re-punctuating the air with brash gossip. Somewhere in the alley a bottle rolls—an exclamation mark pushed downhill by its own hollow.

I’m thinking, absurdly, about the word dilute. How it looks like it should mean “to extinguish light,” to strike a match at the level of photons. Instead it means adding water: the soft betrayal of potency. Like all the times I’ve poured skim milk into espresso courage at 2 a.m. and still dared the night to deliver a revelation.

There’s a pair of shoes drying on the balcony next door—someone rinsed them after a midnight adventure in puddles or nightclub confessions. They drip with the patience of verdicts. Each drop captures an upside-down city: fire escapes, satellite dishes, a cat yawning into the eaves. The cat is real, by the way. Nineteen years old, lopsided whiskers, believes she is the neighborhood’s compliance officer. She let me stroke her once in exchange for a single fingertip of sardine oil; we negotiated a treaty I have since violated dozens of times.

Sometimes I imagine the world is just the afterimage of a much bigger bang that happened in another universe’s yesterday, and we are the smoke ring exhaled by a god who is also a librarian and who filed us under “recreation—miscellaneous.” Somewhere between the dew-laced spiderweb and the screaming kettle, that librarian glances at her watch and decides we can have one more renewal before closing time.

I feel like writing a letter to gravity.

Dear Gravity,  
I know you’re busy keeping oceans from wandering off and tide pompadours pinned to the moon’s scalp,  
but if you could just leave my memories suspended a few extra inches above the floor, maybe I could walk underneath them without bumping my heart so hard.

Would you?

Yours in levity—  
a citizen dangling on the edge of orbit  

If I soak lentils long enough, they tell me histories in Hammurabi’s voice. It’s not a party trick; more like unpaid overtime for legumes. They complain about currency devaluation in the Bronze Age, about garlic that didn’t know when to stop dominating a stew, about women who plucked stars from the sky and re-seeded them as wheat. I nod and float bay leaves across their small dark sea, promising I will bring no cumin tyrants to their parliament today.

The kettle finally consents. Steam blooms, a shy ectoplasm curling around the kitchen like somebody whispering gossip about water. In the window I catch my reflection: hair still doing drunk cartwheels across my forehead, eyes the color of late-storm forest. I look momentarily capable of belief, which is something nobody warns you leaks out quietly after thirty, like a slow puncture in a bike tire you keep meaning to patch but somehow just pump fuller and faster instead.

I think I’ll walk to the river today. If I arrive at exactly 11:07 a.m., I’ll meet the heron who knows Morse code. He taps messages into the surface with one prehistoric foot: half fishing, half prayer, half limerick. Last week he sent

dot dot dash / dot dash dash / dot dot dot  
which, among beaks and ferrules and skimming swallows, translates roughly as  
“Beware napping under cherry trees—the petals will drown you in ammunition of softness.”

I took his advice; instead I napped under a magazine kiosk and dreamed that typewriter keys were migratory birds returning to the Arctic of the alphabet. Woke up with a Q nesting in my hair, telling me winter had been mild up there, mostly semicolons.

I’m out of tea now, spoon scraping china like an archaeologist of emptiness. The day curls at the edges, ready. All the cancelled anniversaries and almost phone calls hover like library fines waiting to be forgiven. I push them aside and open the door. The outside smells of bread about to happen.

There’s a small song, too quiet for microphones, being rehearsed between sunbeams and stray urban weeds. I decide to hum along.
